dao.name: Dao
dao.subtitle: The Way
dao.sancai: Heaven — the way of what is above
dao.triadBlurb: Texts of alignment, action, release, discipline, and forms of life less governed by coercion and fear.
dao.lede: This path concerns alignment before ideology: how one moves, yields, acts, refrains, and learns to live without forcing the world into a shape it will not keep.
dao.statement: Many begin here after discovering that inherited structures gave coherence at the cost of continual inner pressure. The way does not abolish seriousness. It teaches another manner of bearing it.
dao.why: These books belong here because they teach orientation before system. They are concerned not only with what is true, but with how one stands, receives, moves, disciplines attention, and loosens the compulsion to control by force.

core.title: Daodejing
core.note: Dao De Jing: A Philosophical Translation; Making This Life Significant. By Roger T. Ames and David L. Hall.

quote.text: The Way that can be spoken of is not the constant Way.

core.why: The root text of the Way—power through yielding, action through non-coercion. It teaches how to move with the grain of things rather than force the world into a shape it will not keep.

echoes.title: The Complete Zhuangzi
echoes.note: The same Way at play—the holy fool who will not be carved into a useful shape.

echoes.title: The Secret of the Golden Flower
echoes.note: Wu wei turned inward: letting the light circulate of itself rather than forcing it.

echoes.title: The Sermon on the Mount
echoes.note: The lilies that toil not—provision without striving, in the Christian key.

core.title: Shōbōgenzō
core.note: Treasury of the True Dharma Eye: Zen Master Dogen's Shobo Genzo. Edited by Kazuaki Tanahashi.

quote.text: To study the self is to forget the self.

core.why: Dōgen makes practice itself the teaching. The Way is not a doctrine to hold but a manner of sitting, walking, working, and seeing—realization inseparable from the act.

echoes.title: On Having No Head
echoes.note: Realization you can check in the present act—forgetting the self, first-person.

echoes.title: The Gateless Gate
echoes.note: The koan as practice that drops the seeker rather than informing him.

echoes.title: The Analects of Confucius
echoes.note: The Way embodied in conduct and rite—the act itself as the teaching.

core.title: The Sermon on the Mount
core.note: Matthew 5–7; and its Book of Mormon parallel, 3 Nephi 12–14.
core.why: The Way in the Latter-day Saint and Christian key: a discipline of non-grasping trust—provision without anxiety, righteousness without force.

quote.text: Consider the lilies of the field, how they grow; they toil not, neither do they spin.

echoes.title: Daodejing
echoes.note: Wuwei: to act without coercion, as the lilies neither toil nor spin.

echoes.title: The Book of Mormon
echoes.note: The same trust made covenant: provision and deliverance through the Lord.

echoes.title: The Prophet
echoes.note: The inner life of trust and surrender, spoken in image.

further.title: Three Zen Sutras
further.note: The Heart Sutra, The Diamond Sutra, and The Platform Sutra.

quote.text: Form is emptiness, emptiness is form.

further.why: On emptiness and the groundlessness that frees. They dissolve the very footing the anxious self keeps trying to stand on.

echoes.title: The Gateless Gate
echoes.note: Emptiness as the gate that was never barred.

echoes.title: On Having No Head
echoes.note: No-self made first-person and checkable, not merely asserted.

echoes.title: Daodejing
echoes.note: The usefulness of the empty—the hub, the vessel, the room.

further.title: The Gateless Gate
further.note: All 48 koans, with commentary by Ekai, called Mumon.

quote.text: The Great Way has no gate; a thousand roads enter it.

further.why: Forty-eight koans that jam the discursive mind until it lets go. The gate is gateless because the Way was never barred—only mistaken for something to reason past.

echoes.title: Three Zen Sutras
echoes.note: The emptiness the koan keeps pointing at.

echoes.title: Shōbōgenzō
echoes.note: Practice as the dropping of the grasping mind.

echoes.title: The Alan Watts Collection
echoes.note: The trap of the self-grasping thought, loosened in plain speech.

further.title: The Book of Lieh-tzu
further.note: A Taoist classic of spontaneity and ease. Translated by A.C. Graham (or Eva Wong).
further.why: Taoist tales of ease, spontaneity, and the man who forgets himself. Where the Daodejing instructs, Lieh-tzu lets the Way be overheard in story.

echoes.title: Daodejing
echoes.note: The Way instructed, here overheard in story.

echoes.title: The Complete Zhuangzi
echoes.note: Kindred Taoist tale-telling—the wild ease and the laughing sage.

echoes.title: The Prophet
echoes.note: Wisdom carried in parable and image rather than precept.

de.name: De
de.subtitle: The Witness
de.sancai: Human — virtue realized in a person
de.triadBlurb: Texts of subjectivity, consciousness, interior disclosure, symbolic encounter, and first-person transformation.
de.lede: This path concerns first-person life: lived experience before system, consciousness before role, and the disclosure of meaning where it is actually encountered rather than merely inherited.
de.statement: Many come here after discovering that they can repeat a tradition's speech without ever having met its claims inwardly. The witness names the difficult movement from language alone into direct contact, interiority, symbol, and experience.
de.why: These works belong here because they insist that insight must be encountered in the first person. They make room for consciousness, symbol, perception, and the inward labor by which a life becomes more truthful to itself.

core.title: On Having No Head
core.note: Zen and the Rediscovery of the Obvious, by D.E. Harding.
core.why: Harding's experiments turn the abstraction 'no-self' into something you can look and check for yourself, right now—witness before doctrine.

echoes.title: Shōbōgenzō
echoes.note: To forget the self in the act—here made plainly first-person.

echoes.title: Three Zen Sutras
echoes.note: No-self asserted in the sutras, here simply seen.

echoes.title: The Complete Zhuangzi
echoes.note: 'I have lost myself,' says the sage—the dissolving of the separate self.

core.title: The Science of the 1st Person
core.note: Its Principles, Practice, and Potential, by D.E. Harding.
core.why: Harding's fuller method: attention turned around to face its own source. A rigorous, first-person empiricism of awareness.

echoes.title: On Having No Head
echoes.note: The same seeing, given its experiments and proofs.

echoes.title: Shōbōgenzō
echoes.note: To study the self until the self is forgotten.

echoes.title: The Alan Watts Collection
echoes.note: Awareness grown aware of itself, in the Western ear.

core.title: The Prophet
core.why: Gibran speaks the inner life in images—love, work, grief, joy—meeting the reader where meaning is actually felt rather than argued.

echoes.title: The Sermon on the Mount
echoes.note: Trust and love spoken in image rather than precept.

echoes.title: The Book of Lieh-tzu
echoes.note: Wisdom carried in parable and story.

echoes.title: Sri Chaitanya and His Associates
echoes.note: Love made the discipline and the way.

core.title: The Secret of the Golden Flower
core.note: From the TaiShang, Taoist lineage. Translations by Thomas Cleary or Xon Leighton.

quote.text: The Golden Flower is the light; when the light is made to circulate, the energies of heaven and earth crystallize.
quote.cite: The Secret of the Golden Flower

core.why: A Taoist manual of inner light and the circulation of attention—the witness as a practice of turning the gaze inward until it shines.

echoes.title: Daodejing
echoes.note: Wu wei turned inward—letting the light move of itself.

echoes.title: The Science of the 1st Person
echoes.note: Attention turned to face its own source.

echoes.title: Shōbōgenzō
echoes.note: Practice as the light that realization gives.

further.title: The Alan Watts Collection
further.note: Available via the Waking Up app.

quote.text: You are an aperture through which the universe is looking at and exploring itself.

further.why: Watts is the great translator of Eastern interiority for the Western ear—loosening the knot of the separate self with wit and clarity.

echoes.title: On Having No Head
echoes.note: No separate self to defend—seeing it for oneself.

echoes.title: Daodejing
echoes.note: The watercourse way—moving with, not against.

echoes.title: The Gateless Gate
echoes.note: The self-grasping thought, loosened.

further.title: Iron John
further.note: A Book About Men, by Robert Bly.

quote.text: Where a man's wound is, that is where his genius will be.

further.why: Bly reads a single fairy tale as the map of a man's inner initiation—symbol and story as instruments of first-person transformation.

echoes.title: Jesus the Christ
echoes.note: Descent, wound, and return—the redemptive pattern.

echoes.title: The Prophet
echoes.note: The inner life told as story and image.

zion.name: Zion
zion.subtitle: The Order
zion.sancai: Earth — the order built among a people
zion.triadBlurb: Texts of covenant, law, communal memory, ritual form, and the difficult labor of making a people.
zion.lede: This path concerns durable form: covenant, law, ritual, memory, and the difficult labor by which persons and peoples are gathered into a life that can be held, transmitted, and judged.
zion.statement: Many resist order after experiencing its anxious distortions. Yet no life remains human for long without form, memory, and shared obligation. Zion names the problem of order recovered rather than merely obeyed.
zion.why: These texts belong here because they are preoccupied with communal life: memory, law, ritual, transmission, hierarchy, covenant, and the formation of a people. They preserve the question of what order can be when it is not reduced to mere control.

core.title: The Book of Mormon
core.note: The Earliest Text. Edited by Royal Skousen.

quote.text: Adam fell that men might be; and men are, that they might have joy.

core.why: A people's covenant record—rise, forgetting, and return. Zion as a thing to be built, lost, and rebuilt across generations.

echoes.title: The Torah
echoes.note: Another covenant record of a people bound by promise.

echoes.title: The Sermon on the Mount
echoes.note: The same Sermon, given again at Bountiful.

echoes.title: Pearl of Great Price
echoes.note: Enoch's Zion—the archetype of the order made whole.

core.title: The Torah
core.note: The foundational text of Judaism — the Pentateuch, first five books of the Hebrew Bible.

quote.text: Hear, O Israel: the Lord our God, the Lord is one.

core.why: The founding law and narrative of a covenant people—how a wandering company becomes an order bound by memory and obligation.

echoes.title: The Book of Mormon
echoes.note: A second witness of covenant and a people.

echoes.title: The Analects of Confucius
echoes.note: Law and rite as the form that holds a people together.

echoes.title: The Sermon on the Mount
echoes.note: The law not abolished but fulfilled and transfigured.

core.title: The Analects of Confucius
core.note: A Philosophical Translation by Roger T. Ames and David L. Hall.

quote.text: Do not impose on others what you yourself do not desire.

core.why: Order as cultivated relation: ritual, role, and reverence as the forms through which a humane society holds together.

echoes.title: The Torah
echoes.note: Law and ritual as the ordering of a people.

echoes.title: Shōbōgenzō
echoes.note: The rite itself as the teaching—the act before the doctrine.

echoes.title: The Book of Mormon
echoes.note: A people ordered by covenant and relation.

core.title: The Complete Zhuangzi
core.note: The Complete Works of Zhuangzi. Translated by Burton Watson.

quote.text: Now I do not know whether I was a man dreaming I was a butterfly, or am now a butterfly dreaming I am a man.

core.why: Why on the path of Order? Because every living order needs its holy fool. Zhuangzi is the wild counter-voice within Zion—the laughter that keeps law from hardening into mere rule, and reminds the city that its forms serve life, not the reverse.

echoes.title: Daodejing
echoes.note: The Way the order must not forget—wu wei at play.

echoes.title: The Book of Lieh-tzu
echoes.note: Kindred Taoist tales of ease and the forgotten self.

echoes.title: On Having No Head
echoes.note: 'I have lost myself'—the self that order keeps trying to fix.

further.title: Pearl of Great Price
further.note: by the Prophet Joseph Smith Jr.

quote.text: This is my work and my glory—to bring to pass the immortality and eternal life of man.

further.why: Latter-day scripture of cosmogony and covenant—Enoch's Zion, the city taken up, as the archetype of an order made whole.

echoes.title: The Book of Mormon
echoes.note: The covenant continued; Zion built and gathered.

echoes.title: The Torah
echoes.note: Moses and the creation—the cosmogony of a people.

echoes.title: Daodejing
echoes.note: The generative source from which the worlds proceed.

further.title: Jesus the Christ
further.epithet: The Life That Founds the Covenant
further.why: Talmage’s measured account of the life that, for the Latter-day Saint, founds the new covenant and the order built upon it.

echoes.title: The Book of Mormon
echoes.note: The same Christ, testified by another witness.

echoes.title: The Sermon on the Mount
echoes.note: His teaching—the Way he gave to walk.

echoes.title: Iron John
echoes.note: Descent, wound, and return—the redemptive pattern in story.

further.title: Sri Chaitanya and His Associates
further.why: A devotional order formed around shared ecstatic practice—community as the vessel and discipline of love.

echoes.title: Jesus the Christ
echoes.note: Devotion gathered around a divine person.

echoes.title: The Prophet
echoes.note: Love made the path and the practice.
